- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- datalake-ci-permissions: Use when user asks to add or update any AWS resource deployment or GitLab CI IAM roles scoped to the datalake environment. Do not trigger for non-datalake environments (dev, prod, sandbox, services) unless explicitly requested.
- mr-description: Writes merge request message description when raising a GitLab Merge Request. Use when creating a merge request or when user asks to summarise changes for a merge request.
- commit-message: Generate concise git commit messages for Data Engineering changes. Use PROACTIVELY when the user asks to commit files, create a commit, or generate a git commit message.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# GDPR Right to Erasure Orchestration Platform

This project was originally created to replace the
[Tines](https://www.tines.com/) based Right to Erasure Orchestrator.

## Project Goals

The primary goal of this project is to replace the existing Tines based
orchestrator for subject erasure requests in regards to the GDPR legislation.

The main problems being solved are as followed:

- The costs related to the use of Tines
- The use of Jira as a point of integration and state storage

This project also moves the ownership of the orchestration into the Data
Engineering team.

## Project Details

This project is built using Python 3.13+ and uses
[uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) as a package manager and build tool.

## Development

After cloning this repository you should run:

```sh
uv sync --all-groups
uv run prek install
```

This should create a virtual environment under `.venv` in the local directory,
download all dependencies, and install a Git pre-commit hook.

Generally speaking, if you want to run a normal Python command you should
prefix it with `uv run`.

### Testing

This project uses `pytest` for unit testing. These should be run using the
following command:

```sh
uv run pytest
```

`pytest-cov` is used as an extension that can help you see how much coverage
the tests are actually giving. You can run this as follows:

```sh
uv run pytest --cov=dns_de_rte_orchestrator
```

There isn't a set test coverage percentage code should have, but key files
should generally have more coverage than others.

### Structure

The key entry points for AWS Lambda are:

- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py`
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py`

The storage logic sits primarily in the `RequestRepository` parent class and its
implementations in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py`.

The business logic sits in the `RequestProcessor` class in
`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py`.

### Managing Dependencies

`pip` commands should not be used to manage dependencies, instead you should
use commands such as:

```sh
# To add a library dependency
uv add library
# To add a development dependency only
uv add --dev pytest
# To remove a dependency
uv remove library
```

This should update the `uv.lock` file, but if in doubt you can run `uv lock`
to regenerate it.

You can attempt to upgrade packages using the following `uv` command:

```sh
uv lock --upgrade-package library
```

## Deployment

The code in this project is deployed as 2 separate AWS Lambdas using [AWS
SAM](https://aws.amazon.com/serverless/sam/).

The versions of Python used during development, testing, and deployment should
all match as closely as possible.

GitLab runners are used to execute tests and deployments. The configuration for
this is managed in `.gitlab-ci.yml`.

<!-- TODO: Add deployment details -->

## Code Quality Tools

This project makes use of several tools to ensure consistent code quality.

You can run these tools locally alongside `pytest` by running:

```sh
./local-ci.sh
```

### prek

[prek](https://prek.j178.dev/) is a replacement for the
[pre-commit](https://pre-commit.com/) framework. Both allow you to run scripts
and checks before a Git commit is created.

`prek` is primarily used to run the other code quality tools and ensure that
the repository remains in a high quality state.

You will need to install the Git pre-commit hooks once after cloning the
repository by running:

```sh
uv run prek install
```

This should force `prek` to run before all local commits.

You can also manually run `prek` by using:

```sh
uv run prek
```

The `prek` configuration is stored in `prek.toml`. It configures many
pre-commit checks and runs `ruff`, `basedpyright` and `black`.

### ruff

[ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/) is both a fast linter AND code formatter
for Python. This project primarily uses it to lint code.

You can run it via:

```sh
uv run ruff check
```

This can prevent common Python mistakes.

### basedpyright

[basedpyright](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/) is a fork of `pyright`.
It works as a static type-checker for Python code.

You should include type hints on as much code as possible to help it out and
manage programmer expectations. You should also avoid using deprecated types
along with the `Any` type when possible.

You can manually run `basedpyright` using `uv`:

```sh
uv run basedpyright
```

Sometimes libraries may not include enough type information for `basedpyright`.
In these instances you can create type stubs. This process can be done by hand,
or you can try to use `basedpyright` to generate said stubs for you via a
command like:

```sh
uv run basedpyright --createstub library
```

These stubs would be placed in the `typings/` directory at the root of the
project.

Sometimes type stubs may not be enough, in this case you can add [ignore
comments](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/configuration/comments/) to the
code.

```py
# pyright: ignore
```

You can also edit the `pyrightconfig.json` and add specific libraries to
`allowedUntypedLibraries` to allow a whole library to be untyped.

#### boto3

For `boto3` type stubs there is the package
[boto3-stubs](https://pypi.org/project/boto3-stubs/) which suggests generating
them yourself using a call similar to:

```sh
uvx --with 'boto3==1.42.92' mypy-boto3-builder
```

These have been stored in the `vendored/` directory.

### black

[black](https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) is a code formatter for
Python.

You can run `black` directly on a specific file using:

```sh
uv run black path/to/file.py
```

`black` is used to ensure all Python code follows the same standard of
formatting.

from dataclasses import dataclass, fields
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Literal, override

type ErasureState = Literal[
    "new", "in-progress", "phase-1", "phase-2", "phase-3", "done", "need-more-detail"
]
"""Valid erasure states type"""

type ErasureDetailKey = Literal[
    "first-name", "last-name", "email", "postcode", "phone-number", "rfl", "last-order"
]
"""Expected erasure details keys"""

VALID_STATES: tuple[ErasureState, ...] = (
    "new",
    "in-progress",
    "phase-1",
    "phase-2",
    "phase-3",
    "done",
    "need-more-detail",
)
"""Valid erasure states"""


@dataclass
class ErasureRequest:
    """
    An Erasure request as submitted by a source system.

    The normal source system is OneTrust.
    """

    id: str
    """
    A unique identifier for this erasure request.
    This should come from the source system.
    """

    source: str
    """The source of this Erasure request"""

    state: ErasureState
    """The state that the request is in"""

    date_issued: datetime
    """The date (and time) that this request was issued at"""

    # TODO: Perhaps turn erasure_details into its own domain object
    erasure_details: dict[ErasureDetailKey, str]
    """
    Specific details for this erasure.

    This is a sensitive field as it will contain the likes of email addresses,
    addresses etc.

    The erasure details we normally get from OneTrust are:

    - First Name
    - Last Name
    - Email
    - Postcode

    With the optional ones being:

    - Phone Number
    - RFL Number
    - Last Order Number
    """

    # Below code attempts to omit the erasure_details from inclusing in strings
    # by mistake

    def _safe_dict(self) -> dict[str, str]:
        return {
            f.name: getattr(self, f.name)
            for f in fields(self)
            if f.name not in ["erasure_details"]
        }

    @override
    def __str__(self):
        d = self._safe_dict()
        return f"ErasureRequest(id={d['id']}, source={d['source']}, state={d['state']}, date_issued={d['date_issued']})"

    @override
    def __repr__(self):
        d = self._safe_dict()
        return f"ErasureRequest(**{d})"

    @property
    def date_issued_ms(self) -> int:
        """The date and time issued as a unix timestamp (milliseconds)"""
        timestamp_ms = int(self.date_issued.timestamp() * 1000)
        return timestamp_ms

18	    """Common interface for dealing with Erasure Request storage"""
19	
20	    @abstractmethod
21	    def create(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
22	        """Create a new erasure request in the underlying storage"""
23	        raise NotImplementedError()
24	
25	    @abstractmethod
26	    def update(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
27	        """
28	        Updates an existing item in the underlying storage based on the given request.
29

31	        """

36	        """Get a specific request from the underlying storage based upon its id."""
37	        raise NotImplementedError()

42	        state: ErasureState,
43	        limit: int = 100,
44	        from_date: datetime | None = None,
45	    ) -> list[ErasureRequest]:
46	        """
47	        Get the erasure requests within a given state up to a certain limit.
48	
49	        When the limit is reached, that many items are returned. There could be
50	        more items with the given state.
51	
52	        You can optionally set a from_date to filter only results from that
53	        given time and date until now.
54	
55	        Warning: This can return a lot of results if you use a terminal state.
56	
57	        Warning: Implementations may cap the amount of items returned under the
58	        given limit if certain conditions are met. For example, DynamoDB will
59	        stop returning items for a single query if the total size reaches 1 MB.
60	        It is up to implementations as to how much they respect this limit.
61	        For example, a DynamoDB implementation could paginate.
62	
63	        """
64	        raise NotImplementedError()

67	class InMemoryRequestRepository(RequestRepository):
68	    """
69	    An example implementation of a request repository.
70	
71	    This implementation simply stores objects in an internal dictionary.
72	    It does not clone objects.
73	
74	    This implementation is not thread safe.
75	    """
76	
77	    def __init__(self) -> None:
78	        super().__init__()
79	        self._db: dict[str, ErasureRequest] = {}
80	
81	    @override
82	    def create(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
83	        if request.id in self._db:
84	            raise ValueError(f"{request.id} already exists")
85	        self._db[request.id] = request
86	
87	    @override
88	    def update(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
89	        if request.id not in self._db:
90	            raise ValueError(f"{request.id} does not already exists")
91	        self._db[request.id] = request
92	
93	    @override
94	    def get(self, id: str) -> ErasureRequest | None:
95	        return self._db.get(id)
96	
97	    @override
98	    def get_all_in_state(
99	        self, state: ErasureState, limit: int = 100, from_date: datetime | None = None
100	    ) -> list[ErasureRequest]:
101	        if limit <= 0:
102	            return []
103	        found: list[ErasureRequest] = []
104	        count: int = 0
105	        for _, item in self._db.items():
106	            if item.state == state:
107	                if from_date is not None and item.date_issued < from_date:
108	                    continue
109	                found.append(item)
110	                count += 1
111	                if count >= limit:
112	                    break
113	        return found

116	class DynamoDBRequestRepository(RequestRepository):
117	    """DynamoDB backed repository implementation"""
118	
119	    ID_FIELD: Literal["id"] = "id"
120	    STATE_FIELD: Literal["state"] = "state"
121	    DATE_ISSUED_FIELD: Literal["date_issued"] = "date_issued"
122	    SOURCE_FIELD: Literal["source"] = "source"
123	    ERASURE_DETAILS_FIELD: Literal["erasure_details"] = "erasure_details"
124	
125	    NOT_EXISTS_CONDITION: str = f"attribute_not_exists({ID_FIELD})"
126	    EXISTS_CONDITION: str = f"attribute_exists({ID_FIELD})"
127	
128	    def __init__(
129	        self, table_name: str, index_name: str, dynamodb: DynamoDBClient
130	    ) -> None:
131	        super().__init__()
132	        self.table_name: str = table_name
133	        self.index_name: str = index_name
134	        self.dynamodb: DynamoDBClient = dynamodb
135	
136	    @override
137	    def create(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
138	        item = self.item_from_request(request)
139	        # This should error if there is an item in the table with the given id
140	        _response = self.dynamodb.put_item(
141	            TableName=self.table_name,
142	            Item=item,
143	            ReturnValues="NONE",
144	            ConditionExpression=self.NOT_EXISTS_CONDITION,
145	        )
146	
147	    @override
148	    def update(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
149	        item = self.item_from_request(request)
150	        # This should error if there is NOT an item in the table with the given
151	        # id
152	        _response = self.dynamodb.put_item(
153	            TableName=self.table_name,
154	            Item=item,
155	            ReturnValues="NONE",
156	            ConditionExpression=self.EXISTS_CONDITION,
157	        )
158	
159	    @override
160	    def get(self, id: str) -> ErasureRequest | None:
161	        response = self.dynamodb.get_item(
162	            TableName=self.table_name, Key={self.ID_FIELD: {"S": id}}
163	        )
164	
165	        if "Item" in response:
166	            item: dict[str, AttributeValueTypeDef] = response["Item"]
167	            # Convert the item back to a domain object
168	            return self.response_to_request(item)
169	        else:
170	            return None
171	
172	    @override
173	    def get_all_in_state(
174	        self,
175	        state: ErasureState,
176	        limit: int = 100,
177	        from_date: datetime | None = None,
178	    ) -> list[ErasureRequest]:
179	        if limit <= 0:
180	            return []
181	        key_condition: str = "#S = :state"
182	
183	        #  The attribute values being used in the query
184	        attribute_values: Mapping[str, UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef] = {
185	            ":state": {"S": state}
186	        }
187	        # Replacements for attribute names that are reserved keywords
188	        attribute_names: Mapping[str, str] = {"#S": "state"}
189	
190	        if from_date is not None:
191	            # Add on the sort key condition to the key_condition
192	            key_condition += " AND date_issued >= :date"
193	            # Add the sort key value to attribute_values
194	            timestamp = int(from_date.timestamp() * 1000)
195	            attribute_values[":date"] = {"N": str(timestamp)}
196	
197	        paginator = self.dynamodb.get_paginator("query")
198	        pages = paginator.paginate(
199	            TableName=self.table_name,
200	            IndexName=self.index_name,
201	            Select="ALL_ATTRIBUTES",
202	            ScanIndexForward=True,  # explicitly set direction of scan
203	            KeyConditionExpression=key_condition,
204	            ExpressionAttributeNames=attribute_names,
205	            ExpressionAttributeValues=attribute_values,
206	            PaginationConfig={"PageSize": min([100, limit]), "MaxItems": limit},
207	        )
208	
209	        results: list[ErasureRequest] = []
210	        for page in pages:
211	            # iterate pages and add converted results to list
212	            if "Items" not in page:
213	                continue
214	            for item in page["Items"]:
215	                erasure = self.response_to_request(item)
216	                results.append(erasure)
217	
218	        return results
219	
220	    @classmethod
221	    def response_to_request(
222	        cls, response: dict[str, AttributeValueTypeDef]
223	    ) -> ErasureRequest:
224	        """Convert a response dictionary to an ErasureRequest"""
225	        if cls.ID_FIELD not in response:
226	            raise ValueError(f"Missing {cls.ID_FIELD} field")
227	        if cls.STATE_FIELD not in response:
228	            raise ValueError(f"Missing {cls.STATE_FIELD} field")
229	        if cls.DATE_ISSUED_FIELD not in response:
230	            raise ValueError(f"Missing {cls.DATE_ISSUED_FIELD} field")
231	        if cls.SOURCE_FIELD not in response:
232	            raise ValueError(f"Missing {cls.SOURCE_FIELD} field")
233	
234	        id: str = response[cls.ID_FIELD][
235	            "S"
236	        ]  # pyright: ignore[reportTypedDictNotRequiredAccess]
237	        state: str = response[cls.STATE_FIELD][
238	            "S"
239	        ]  # pyright: ignore[reportTypedDictNotRequiredAccess]
240	        source: str = response[cls.SOURCE_FIELD][
241	            "S"
242	        ]  # pyright: ignore[reportTypedDictNotRequiredAccess]
243	
244	        date_issued_field = response[cls.DATE_ISSUED_FIELD]
245	        if "N" not in date_issued_field:
246	            raise ValueError(f"Wrong type for {cls.DATE_ISSUED_FIELD}")
247	        date_issued_ms = int(date_issued_field["N"])
248	        date_issued: datetime = datetime.fromtimestamp(date_issued_ms / 1000)
249	
250	        erasure_details: dict[str, str] = {}
251	        if cls.ERASURE_DETAILS_FIELD in response:
252	            erasure_details_field = response[cls.ERASURE_DETAILS_FIELD]
253	            if "M" in erasure_details_field:
254	                returned_map = erasure_details_field["M"]
255	                for k, v in returned_map.items():  # pyright: ignore[reportAny]
256	                    coerced = cast(dict[str, str], v)
257	                    value: str = coerced["S"]
258	                    erasure_details[k] = value
259	
260	        # TODO: Should we validate the fields afterward?
261	        return ErasureRequest(
262	            id=id,
263	            state=state,  # pyright: ignore[reportArgumentType]
264	            date_issued=date_issued,
265	            source=source,
266	            erasure_details=erasure_details,  # pyright: ignore[reportArgumentType]
267	        )
268	
269	    @classmethod
270	    def item_from_request(cls, request: ErasureRequest):
271	        """Convert a request to a DynamoDB Item"""
272	        # Build the map type
273	        erasure_details: dict[str, dict[str, str]] = {}
274	        for k, v in request.erasure_details.items():
275	            erasure_details[k] = {"S": v}
276	
277	        item = {
278	            cls.ID_FIELD: {"S": request.id},
279	            cls.STATE_FIELD: {"S": request.state.strip().lower()},
280	            cls.DATE_ISSUED_FIELD: {"N": str(request.date_issued_ms)},
281	            cls.SOURCE_FIELD: {"S": request.source},
282	            cls.ERASURE_DETAILS_FIELD: {"M": erasure_details},
283	        }
284	        return item

307	    """Return a DynamoDB request repository from using environment variables"""
308

316

318	    return DynamoDBRequestRepository(
319	        table_name=table_name, index_name=index_name, dynamodb=dynamodb
320	    )

324	"""The environment variable used to force a specific repository type"""
325	
326	
327	def get_request_repository() -> RequestRepository:
328	    """Get a concrete repository via the environment variables"""
329	    repo_type: str | None = os.getenv(REPOSITORY_TYPE_ENV_VAR)
330	
331	    match repo_type:
332	        case "" | None:
333	            return _get_default_request_repository()
334	        case s:
335	            match s.strip().lower():

from typing import Any

from aws_lambda_powertools import Logger
from aws_lambda_powertools.tracing import Tracer
from aws_lambda_powertools.utilities.typing import LambdaContext
from dotenv import load_dotenv

from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.erasure_request import ErasureRequest

from .request_repository import RequestRepository, get_request_repository

# Load .env file, mostly useful for testing
_ = load_dotenv()

logger = Logger()
tracer = Tracer()  # Sets service via POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME env var

request_repo: RequestRepository = get_request_repository()


def request_from_event(
    _event: dict[Any, Any],  # pyright: ignore[reportExplicitAny]
) -> ErasureRequest:
    """Convert the input event into an ErasureRequest"""
    # TODO: Implement erasure request conversion
    raise NotImplementedError("Request conversion not implemented")


@tracer.capture_lambda_handler
@logger.inject_lambda_context
def lambda_handler(
    event: dict[Any, Any],  # pyright: ignore[reportExplicitAny]
    _context: LambdaContext,
) -> None:
    """
    Entrypoint for AWS Lambda that does item creation.

    This should be very simple, the Lambda should simply create an entry in the
    underlying storage, another lambda or piece of compute will process them.
    """

    request: ErasureRequest = request_from_event(event)
    request_repo.create(request)

12	logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

14	
15	class RequestProcessor:
16	    """Container for Erasure Request processing logic"""
17	
18	    PROCESS_STATES: tuple[ErasureState, ...] = (
19	        "new",
20	        "in-progress",
21	        "phase-1",
22	        "phase-2",
23	        "phase-3",
24	    )
25	    """The list of states (in order) that will have items processed"""
26	
27	    def __init__(self, repository: RequestRepository) -> None:
28	        self.repo: RequestRepository = repository
29	
30	    def _check_timeout(self, timeout: datetime | None) -> bool:
31	        if timeout is None:
32	            return False
33	        now: datetime = datetime.now()
34	        return now >= timeout
35	
36	    def process(self, timeout: datetime | None = None):
37	        """
38	        Process the requests with an optional timeout to bail early before.
39	
40	        When working in a time limited environment like AWS Lambda, this
41	        timeout should be set a little before the whole Lambda will expire to
42	        account for interrupting the running logic.
43	
44	        The only time an error should be raised related to the timeout is if we
45	        timeout before the process has even begun as this indicates too short a
46	        timeout.
47	        """
48	
49	        if timeout is not None:
50	            logger.info("Process triggered with timeout of %s", timeout.isoformat())
51	        else:
52	            logger.info("Process triggered without timeout")
53	
54	        # This should do the following:
55	        # - Get all requests in a non-terminal state
56	        # - process each request appropriatelty
57	        # It should do this for each different non-terminal state in turn and
58	        # be checking for time remaining in-between each to make sure that we
59	        # have enough time left for this invocation, since this could be running
60	        # in an AWS Lambda.
61	        # We can also happily continue at the next function call since we will
62	        # be running often
63	
64	        if self._check_timeout(timeout):
65	            # Timed out before we began
66	            # This isn't right so raise an error
67	            raise ValueError("We timed out before we began")
68	
69	        # NOTE: We could improve the timeout logic by timning each previous
70	        # process. We'd then use this data to estimate if we'd timeout rather
71	        # than using a safety margin
72	
73	        # Collect some stats on the process
74	        failed_request_count: int = 0
75	        successful_request_count: int = 0
76	        processed_request_count: int = 0
77	        timed_out: bool = False
78	
79	        # NOTE: When we get all those requests in a given state, we may
80	        # well end up processing the same request through multiple states
81	        # if it has been transitioned to the next AND the storage layer has
82	        # consistently updated.
83	        for state in self.PROCESS_STATES:
84	            # Stop early if we've timed out
85	            # We can process this state in another iteration
86	            if self._check_timeout(timeout):
87	                logger.info('Timed out before checking state "%s"', state)
88	                timed_out = True

import logging
from abc import ABC
from datetime import datetime

from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.erasure_request import (
    VALID_STATES,
    ErasureRequest,
    ErasureState,
)
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.request_repository import RequestRepository

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class RequestProcessor:
    """Container for Erasure Request processing logic"""

    PROCESS_STATES: tuple[ErasureState, ...] = (
        "new",
        "in-progress",
        "phase-1",
        "phase-2",
        "phase-3",
    )
    """The list of states (in order) that will have items processed"""

    def __init__(self, repository: RequestRepository) -> None:
        self.repo: RequestRepository = repository

    def _check_timeout(self, timeout: datetime | None) -> bool:
        if timeout is None:
            return False
        now: datetime = datetime.now()
        return now >= timeout

    def process(self, timeout: datetime | None = None):
        """
        Process the requests with an optional timeout to bail early before.

        When working in a time limited environment like AWS Lambda, this
        timeout should be set a little before the whole Lambda will expire to
        account for interrupting the running logic.

        The only time an error should be raised related to the timeout is if we
        timeout before the process has even begun as this indicates too short a
        timeout.
        """

        if timeout is not None:
            logger.info("Process triggered with timeout of %s", timeout.isoformat())
        else:
            logger.info("Process triggered without timeout")

        # This should do the following:
        # - Get all requests in a non-terminal state
        # - process each request appropriatelty
        # It should do this for each different non-terminal state in turn and
        # be checking for time remaining in-between each to make sure that we
        # have enough time left for this invocation, since this could be running
        # in an AWS Lambda.
        # We can also happily continue at the next function call since we will
        # be running often

        if self._check_timeout(timeout):
            # Timed out before we began
            # This isn't right so raise an error
            raise ValueError("We timed out before we began")

        # NOTE: We could improve the timeout logic by timning each previous
        # process. We'd then use this data to estimate if we'd timeout rather
        # than using a safety margin

        # Collect some stats on the process
        failed_request_count: int = 0
        successful_request_count: int = 0
        processed_request_count: int = 0
        timed_out: bool = False

        # NOTE: When we get all those requests in a given state, we may
        # well end up processing the same request through multiple states
        # if it has been transitioned to the next AND the storage layer has
        # consistently updated.
        for state in self.PROCESS_STATES:
            # Stop early if we've timed out
            # We can process this state in another iteration
            if self._check_timeout(timeout):
                logger.info('Timed out before checking state "%s"', state)
                timed_out = True
                break

            all_in_state = self.repo.get_all_in_state(state)
            logger.info('Found %d entries for state "%s"', len(all_in_state), state)

            for request in all_in_state:
                # Stop processing the items if we've timed out
                # We can pick back up in the next iteration
                if self._check_timeout(timeout):
                    logger.info(
                        'Timed out while checking state "%s" before request: %s',
                        state,
                        request,
                    )
                    timed_out = True
                    break
                # Process the single erasure request
                # We aren't using batch operations here so that we can be
                # idempotent as it relates to each individual request
                processed_request_count += 1
                try:
                    # We do this in a try block as we don't want the whole
                    # process to fail if individual requests do
                    self.process_request(request)
                    successful_request_count += 1
                except ErasureRequestException:
                    logger.exception("Problem processing request %s", request.id)
                    failed_request_count += 1

        # After all the processing has been completed we need to quickly do
        # alerts as we may have timed out
        if processed_request_count > 0:
            logger.info(
                "Processed %d/%d requests successfully",
                successful_request_count,
                processed_request_count,
            )
            if failed_request_count >= processed_request_count:
                # Raise an exception if all processing failed.
                # This could be due to some misconfiguration so the calling
                # code should be alerted
                raise AllProcessingFailed(failed_request_count)
            if timed_out:
                # This logging message is partly redundent but if we often
                # time out it might be a bad sign
                logger.warning("Timed out while processing")
            if failed_request_count > 0:
                logger.error("%d requests failed to process", failed_request_count)
        else:
            logger.info("No requests processed")
            if timed_out:
                logger.warning("We timed out while checking states without finding any")

    def process_request(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        """Process a single request"""
        # This is where the meat of the business logic should be
        logger.info("Processing request: %s", request)

        # Defensively align the state to a standard lower case format
        request_state: str = request.state.strip().lower()
        if request_state not in VALID_STATES:
            raise InvalidRequestState(request)

        cast_request_state: ErasureState = request_state
        match cast_request_state:
            case "new":
                self.process_new(request)
            case "in-progress":
                self.process_in_progress(request)
            case "phase-1":
                self.process_phase1(request)
            case "phase-2":
                self.process_phase2(request)
            case "phase-3":
                self.process_phase3(request)
            case _:
                raise NotImplementedError(
                    f'Request processing of state "{request_state}" not implemented'
                )

    @classmethod
    def _validate_request(
        cls, current_state: ErasureState, request: ErasureRequest
    ) -> None:
        """
        Validate a request for the given current state.

        This will raise an exception if the request is not valid.
        """
        if request.state != current_state:
            raise WrongStateForProcess(request, current_state)

    def process_new(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        self._validate_request("new", request)
        logger.info("Processing new request %s", request.id)

        # Check if there is an email attached to the request
        email: str | None = request.erasure_details.get("email")

        if email is None or email.strip() == "":
            # Missing email address, this should always be present as the data
            # privacy team verify this in OneTrust before the request reaches
            # our system

            logger.warning(
                "Request %s has no email so will be transitioned to need-more-detail",
                request.id,
            )
            # Update the request before raising an exception
            request.state = "need-more-detail"
            self.repo.update(request)

            raise MissingEmailAddress(request)  # TODO: Should we raise?

        # TODO: Update OneTrust in some way to tell it that we've started to
        # process a given request

        logger.info("Transitioning request %s to in-progress", request.id)
        request.state = "in-progress"
        self.repo.update(request)

    def process_in_progress(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        self._validate_request("in-progress", request)

        # TODO: In the original process this "in-progress" stage did not do a
        # great deal

        # Enrich the request if possible or required
        self.enrich_request(request)

        # TODO: Update OneTrust in some way to tell it that we've moved to the
        # next phase

        # Move to the next state
        request.state = "phase-1"
        self.repo.update(request)

    def process_phase1(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        self._validate_request("phase-1", request)
        logger.info("Running request %s through Phase-1", request.id)
        # This is the legacy system stage
        # For now all this should do is notify the AS400 team via both Slack
        # AND Microsoft Teams
        self.notify_as400_team(request)

        # TODO: Update OneTrust that phase-1 was done

        logger.info("Transitioning request %s to Phase-2", request.id)
        request.state = "phase-2"
        self.repo.update(request)

    def process_phase2(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        # This is where the core of erasure logic happened in Tines
        self._validate_request("phase-2", request)

        # TODO: Update OneTrust throughout each part of phase-2

        # TODO: Subflow 1 from Tines:
        # TODO: Search for the customer in All Sites using the Customer API
        # TODO: Submit an erasure to the Customer Erasures API
        # If we found the sites for a customer we include those, otherwise it
        # is not included

        # TODO: Subflow 2 from Tines:
        # TODO: Check the Customer Erasures API Status for the Erasure Request
        # If it's successful we then transition to the next subflow

        # TODO: Subflow 3 from Tines:
        # TODO: Search and Delete Zendesk Tickets

        # TODO: Subflow 4 from Tines:
        # TODO: Search and delete Zendesk users

        # TODO: Subflow 5 from Tines:
        # TODO: Purge the deleted Zendesk tickets

        # TODO: Subflow 6 from Tines:
        # TODO: Purge the deleted Zendesk users

        # Finally after all this we transition to the next stage

        raise NotImplementedError("phase-2 Request processing not implemented")

    def process_phase3(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        self._validate_request("phase-3", request)
        # TODO: Phase-3 from Tines
        # This is just checking if the customer still appears in the customer
        # API or not
        # TODO: What should we do if the previous erasure states all failed?

        # Finally transition to done
        raise NotImplementedError("phase-3 Request processing not implemented")

    def enrich_request(self, _request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        """Enrich the erasure request if needed with details from other sources"""
        # Enrichment could include getting more details on the customer that
        # will be needed by downstream erasure processes
        raise NotImplementedError("request enrichmnent not implemented")

    def notify_as400_team(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        logger.info("Notifying AS400 of request %s", request.id)
        raise NotImplementedError("Notifying AS400 team")

    def send_status_update(self, request: ErasureRequest, update: str) -> None:
        """Send a status update to OneTrust"""
        logger.info("Sending update to OneTrust for %s: %s", request.id, update)
        raise NotImplementedError("Sending status update to OneTrust not implemented")


class AllProcessingFailed(Exception):

    def __init__(self, count: int) -> None:
        self.count: int = count
        super().__init__(f"Processing all {count} requests failed")


class ErasureRequestException(Exception, ABC):
    """Parent exception for Erasure Request process"""

    def __init__(self, request: ErasureRequest, message: str) -> None:
        self.request: ErasureRequest = request
        super().__init__(message)


class InvalidRequestState(ErasureRequestException):
    """Exception raised when a request is not in a valid state"""

    def __init__(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        self.state: str = request.state
        super().__init__(
            request=request,
            message=f"Request {request.id} is in an invalid state: {self.state}",
        )


class WrongStateForProcess(ErasureRequestException):
    """Exception raised when a request is in the wrong state for the given process."""

    def __init__(self, request: ErasureRequest, expected: ErasureState) -> None:
        self.state: str = request.state
        super().__init__(
            request=request,
            message=f"Invalid state on request {request.id} for process: {self.state} != {expected}",
        )


class MissingEmailAddress(ErasureRequestException):
    """Exception raised if the request is missing an email address"""

    def __init__(self, request: ErasureRequest) -> None:
        super().__init__(
            request=request, message=f"Missing email address on request {request.id}"
        )

import os
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from typing import Any, Literal

from aws_lambda_powertools import Logger
from aws_lambda_powertools.tracing import Tracer
from aws_lambda_powertools.utilities.typing import LambdaContext
from dotenv import load_dotenv

from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.request_processor import RequestProcessor
from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.request_repository import (
    RequestRepository,
    get_request_repository,
)

SAFETY_MARGIN_MS_ENV_VAR: Literal["PROCESS_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS"] = (
    "PROCESS_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS"
)
DEFAULT_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS: Literal[1000] = 1000

# Load .env file, mostly useful for testing
_ = load_dotenv()

logger = Logger()
tracer = Tracer()  # Sets service via POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME env var

request_repo: RequestRepository = get_request_repository()
processor: RequestProcessor = RequestProcessor(request_repo)


def get_safety_margin_ms() -> int:
    """Get the safety margin in milliseconds from the environment"""
    from_env = os.getenv("PROCESS_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS")
    safety_margin_ms: int = DEFAULT_SAFETY_MARGIN_MS
    if from_env is not None:
        try:
            safety_margin_ms = int(from_env)
        except ValueError:
            logger.error(
                "Could not parse %s value %s as an integer",
                SAFETY_MARGIN_MS_ENV_VAR,
                from_env,
            )
    return safety_margin_ms


def get_timeout_datetime(remaining_millis: int) -> datetime:
    """Convert a remaining millis into a datetime"""
    delta = timedelta(milliseconds=remaining_millis)
    return datetime.now() + delta


@tracer.capture_lambda_handler
@logger.inject_lambda_context
def lambda_handler(
    _event: dict[Any, Any],  # pyright: ignore[reportExplicitAny]
    context: LambdaContext,
) -> None:
    """
    Entrypoint for AWS Lambda that does erasure request processing.
    """

    # Add a margin for safety so Lambdas don't timeout mid process
    safety_margin_ms: int = get_safety_margin_ms()

    remaining_millis = context.get_remaining_time_in_millis() - safety_margin_ms
    timeout: datetime = get_timeout_datetime(remaining_millis)
    logger.info(
        f"Processing events with a timeout of {remaining_millis}ms until {timeout}"
    )

    # Actual processing logic should be isolated in the processor class
    processor.process(timeout)

139	            email: str = ""

141	            mobile_number: str | None = None

143	            och_id: str | None = None

148	                )

150	                last_name = cast(str | None, personal_details.get("lastName"))
151	                email = cast(str, personal_details.get("email"))
152	                site_id = cast(int | None, personal_details.get("siteId"))
153	                mobile_number = cast(str | None, personal_details.get("mobileNumber"))
154	            if "customerMetadata" in entry:
155	                customer_metadata = cast(
156	                    dict[str, str | bool | None], entry["customerMetadata"]
157	                )
158	                digital_identity_id = cast(
159	                    str | None, customer_metadata.get("digitalIdentityId")
160	                )
161	                och_id = cast(str | None, customer_metadata.get("ochId"))
162	                och_id = cast(str | None, customer_metadata.get("ochId"))
163	            if "loyaltyDetails" in entry:

166	                )

Here's a thorough breakdown of the project:

---

## What it is

This is a **GDPR Right to Erasure (RTE) Orchestrator** — a replacement for a Tines-based automation tool. When customers submit a "right to be forgotten" request (via OneTrust), this system orchestrates the multi-phase deletion of their data across various internal systems. It runs as two AWS Lambda functions.

---

## Overall Design

```
OneTrust (source)
     │
     ▼
[Creator Lambda]  ──── writes ───→  DynamoDB  (state store)
                                        │
                              [Processor Lambda]  (runs on a schedule)
                                        │
                              RequestProcessor
                                        │
                    ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┐
                  new  →  in-progress  →  phase-1  →  phase-2  →  phase-3  →  done
```

**Two Lambda entry points:**
- [creator_lambda.py](src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py) — receives an inbound event (from OneTrust) and creates a new erasure request record in DynamoDB
- [processor_lambda.py](src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py) — triggered on a schedule, processes all outstanding requests through their state machine, respecting a Lambda timeout safety margin

**Core domain objects:**
- [erasure_request.py](src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/erasure_request.py) — `ErasureRequest` dataclass with privacy-safe `__str__`/`__repr__` (masks `erasure_details` from logs)
- [request_repository.py](src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py) — abstract `RequestRepository` with two implementations: `InMemoryRequestRepository` (for tests) and `DynamoDBRequestRepository`
- [request_processor.py](src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py) — `RequestProcessor` drives the state machine; iterates through states, processes each request, handles timeouts gracefully
- [customer_api_client.py](src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/customer_api_client.py) — `HTTPCustomerAPIClient` queries an internal Customer API to look up customers by email across sites (UK, Ireland, Netherlands, etc.)

**State machine:**
`new` → `in-progress` → `phase-1` → `phase-2` → `phase-3` → `done`  
Plus a `need-more-detail` terminal-ish state for missing data.

---

## What's Complete

- Full storage layer with DynamoDB (create, update, get, paginated query by state)
- `ErasureRequest` domain model
- Lambda scaffolding for both creator and processor with AWS Lambda Powertools (structured logging, tracing)
- `process_new`: validates email, transitions to `in-progress`
- `process_in_progress`: calls enrichment, transitions to `phase-1`
- `process_phase1`: notifies AS400 team, transitions to `phase-2`
- Customer API client (HTTP implementation with response parsing)
- Timeout-aware processing loop in the processor
- CI/CD (GitLab), code quality tooling (ruff, black, basedpyright, prek)

---

## What's Missing

**Business logic (the most significant gaps):**

1. **`process_phase2`** — the core erasure logic. Six subflows are stubbed with TODOs:
   - Search customer in all sites via Customer API
   - Submit erasure to Customer Erasures API
   - Poll Customer Erasures API for completion
   - Search & delete Zendesk tickets
   - Search & delete Zendesk users
   - Purge deleted Zendesk tickets and users

2. **`process_phase3`** — verification phase: confirm customer no longer appears in the Customer API after erasure

3. **`enrich_request`** — fills in additional customer details (e.g. OCH ID, loyalty number) needed by downstream phases

4. **`notify_as400_team`** — Slack and/or Microsoft Teams notification for the legacy AS400 system team

5. **`send_status_update`** — OneTrust integration to report progress back to the source system

6. **`request_from_event` in creator_lambda.py** — parsing the inbound Lambda event payload into an `ErasureRequest` (the shape of the OneTrust webhook is not yet mapped)

**Infrastructure:**
- No AWS SAM template (`template.yaml`) — the Lambdas can't be deployed yet
- No CI/CD deployment stage in `.gitlab-ci.yml`

**Tests:**
- No tests for `RequestProcessor` business logic
- No tests for `CustomerAPIClient`
- No audit logging (and no tests for it)

Here's what changed in the pull and what remains outstanding:

---

## What was added

- **`template.yaml`** — SAM template now exists with the `CreateFunction` Lambda defined. DynamoDB permissions wired via a custom `ServerlessAccessManager` resource.
- **`samconfig.toml`** — SAM deploy configuration for `dev` and `prod` environments
- **`Makefile`** — build target used by SAM (`BuildMethod: makefile`)
- **`.gitlab-ci.yml`** — expanded significantly with `sam validate`, `sam build`, and `sam deploy` stages for both dev and prod
- Minor update to `request_repository.py`

---

## What's still missing (from TODO.md)

**Business logic (biggest gap):**
- `process_phase1` — notify AS400 team via Slack/Teams (`notify_as400_team` is a stub)
- `process_phase2` — all 6 Tines subflows unimplemented (Customer API search, Customer Erasures API, Zendesk tickets/users delete and purge)

content.text:
The last commit (merged MR !44) added **loyalty card parsing to the Customer API client**. Specifically:

**[customer_api_client.py](src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/customer_api_client.py):**
- Replaced the `# TODO: Add loyalty card(s) to result` stub with real logic
- Filters loyalty cards to only `isActive: true` ones
- If exactly one active card exists, uses its `id` as the `loyalty_number`
- If multiple active cards exist, logs a warning and picks the most recently created one (`datetimeCreated`)
- Added `_convert_datetime()` helper that parses the API's custom datetime format (`%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%f%z`) with an ISO fallback, and logs warnings on parse failure

**[tests/test_customer_api_client.py](tests/test_customer_api_client.py):**
- Updated the existing test to use the new card shape (`id`, `isActive`, `datetimeCreated`) and assert `loyalty_number == "loyalty-789"`
- Added a new test `test_get_customer_details_prefers_most_recent_active_loyalty_card` covering: one inactive card + two active cards → the newer active card wins

1	# AGENTS.md
2	
3	This is a Python project for automating the orchestration of a GDPR right to be
4	forgotten request. Also known as an Erasure Request or Right to Erasure (RTE)
5	Request.
6	
7	This project is built using Python 3.13 and should be deployed to AWS via the
8	`sam` CLI tool.
9	
10	For more detailed information refer to the `README.md` file.
11	
12	## Project Details
13	
14	This project is managed via the `uv` tool.
15	
16	To make sure that all dependencies are installed run:
17	
18	```sh
19	uv sync --all-groups
20	```
21	
22	Never use raw `pip` or `python` commands. Always use the `uv` tool and the
23	managed virtual environment it provides.
24	
25	To add and remove dependencies use:
26	
27	```sh
28	uv add library
29	uv remove library
30	```
31	
32	If a library is only needed during development, you should add `--dev` to the
33	`uv add` command so that it is added the `dev` dependency group.
34	
35	To upgrade an already added library you can use:
36	
37	```sh
38	uv lock --upgrade-package library
39	```
40

# AGENTS.md

This is a Python project for automating the orchestration of a GDPR right to be
forgotten request. Also known as an Erasure Request or Right to Erasure (RTE)
Request.

This project is built using Python 3.13 and should be deployed to AWS via the
`sam` CLI tool.

For more detailed information refer to the `README.md` file.

## Project Details

This project is managed via the `uv` tool.

To make sure that all dependencies are installed run:

```sh
uv sync --all-groups
```

Never use raw `pip` or `python` commands. Always use the `uv` tool and the
managed virtual environment it provides.

To add and remove dependencies use:

```sh
uv add library
uv remove library
```

If a library is only needed during development, you should add `--dev` to the
`uv add` command so that it is added the `dev` dependency group.

To upgrade an already added library you can use:

```sh
uv lock --upgrade-package library
```

### Testing

Ideally, all code should be tested. Unit tests should be placed in the `tests/`
directory and make use of `pytest`. To run tests, always use `uv`:

```sh
uv run pytest
```

If running in a sandbox, you may need to set the `UV_CACHE_DIR` environment
variable in order for tests to run.

### Code Quality

You should run `prek` before making any commits:

```sh
uv run prek
```

This will run code quality tools, whose output you should act upon.

Type checking is encouraged. Type stubs are generally stored in the `typings/`
directory. `boto3` types are specifically provided via a file in the
`vendored/` directory.

### Deployment

Deployment is done using the AWS SAM CLI tool. It should only be done by a
human or the CI/CD pipeline.

To test that building works you should validate `samconfig.toml` and
`template.yaml` with:

```sh
sam validate --lint
```

And then perform a build with:

```sh
sam build
```

A custom `Makefile` is used to package only the needed dependencies and files
as part of the build.

Never run `sam deploy`.

### Project Tracking

The file `TODO.md` contains a checklist of things left to do on the project and
should be kept up to date after making changes.

from typing import Any

from aws_lambda_powertools import Logger
from aws_lambda_powertools.tracing import Tracer
from aws_lambda_powertools.utilities.typing import LambdaContext
from dotenv import load_dotenv

from dns_de_rte_orchestrator.erasure_request import ErasureRequest

from .request_repository import RequestRepository, get_request_repository

# Load .env file, mostly useful for testing
_ = load_dotenv()

logger = Logger()
tracer = Tracer()  # Sets service via POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME env var

request_repo: RequestRepository = get_request_repository()


def request_from_event(
    _event: dict[Any, Any],  # pyright: ignore[reportExplicitAny]
) -> ErasureRequest:
    """Convert the input event into an ErasureRequest"""
    # TODO: Implement erasure request conversion
    raise NotImplementedError("Request conversion not implemented")


@tracer.capture_lambda_handler
@logger.inject_lambda_context
def lambda_handler(
    event: dict[Any, Any],  # pyright: ignore[reportExplicitAny]
    _context: LambdaContext,
) -> None:
    """
    Entrypoint for AWS Lambda that does item creation.

    This should be very simple, the Lambda should simply create an entry in the
    underlying storage, another lambda or piece of compute will process them.
    """

    request: ErasureRequest = request_from_event(event)
    request_repo.create(request)

AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
Description: >
  Right to Erasure Orchestrator.
  Originally a replacement for the Tines Orchestrator, this stack primarily
  consists of 2 AWS Lambdas that communicate with DynamoDB

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Description: Environment
    Type: String
  DynamoDBTable:
    Description: The DynamoDB table to create new RTE requests in
    Type: String
  DynamoDBTableGSI:
    Description: The DynamoDB table GSI to query for RTE requests
    Type: String

Resources:
  ServerlessAccessManager:
    Type: Custom::ServerlessAccessManager
    Properties:
      ServiceToken: !Sub "arn:aws:lambda:eu-west-1:${AWS::AccountId}:function:paas-serverless-access-manager"
      FunctionName: !Sub 'data-rte-orchestrator-create-request-${Environment}'
      VpcExecution: "true"
      DatadogLogging: "true"
      Targets:
        DynamoDBTable:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Table: !Ref DynamoDBTable

  CreateFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Metadata:
      BuildMethod: makefile
    Properties:
      FunctionName: !GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.FunctionName
      CodeUri: ./
      Handler: dns_de_rte_orchestrator.creator_lambda.lambda_handler
      Runtime: python3.13
      Tracing: Active
      Description: Create RTE requests
      MemorySize: 256
      Timeout: 30
      ReservedConcurrentExecutions: 10
      VpcConfig: !GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.VpcConfig
      Role: !GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleArn
      Environment:
        Variables:
          REPOSITORY_TYPE: "dynamodb"
          DYNAMO_DB_TABLE: !Ref DynamoDBTable
          DYNAMO_DB_INDEX: !Ref DynamoDBTableGSI
          POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME: !Sub "create-erasure-request-${Environment}"

options.description: Versioned path, leaves room for future breaking changes to the payload shape.

options.label: POST /
options.description: Root path. Simple but leaves no room for expanding the API.

input.content:
# Add API Gateway in front of Creator Lambda

## Context

Today the Creator Lambda ([src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py](src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py)) is defined in [template.yaml](template.yaml) but has **no invocation source** — nothing is wired to trigger it. The system is meant to replace a Tines-based orchestrator where OneTrust submits GDPR Right‑to‑Erasure requests over HTTP.

This change adds an **AWS API Gateway REST API** in front of `CreateFunction` so OneTrust (and any future HTTP client) can POST a new erasure request and have it written to DynamoDB. API key authentication is required because the endpoint is internet‑reachable and accepts PII.

Out of scope (existing, unrelated TODOs): implementing `request_from_event`, audit logging, Process Lambda wiring.

## Approach

Add a single `AWS::Serverless::Api` resource to [template.yaml](template.yaml) and attach an API event source to the existing `CreateFunction`. Configure API key auth via a usage plan. Expose `POST /erasure-request`.

### Changes to [template.yaml](template.yaml)

1. **New `Parameters`** (optional, scoped small):
   - None strictly required — reuse existing `Environment`.

2. **New resource: `CreateApi`** (`AWS::Serverless::Api`)
   - `StageName: !Ref Environment` (e.g. `dev`, `prod`)
   - `EndpointConfiguration: REGIONAL` (consistent with `eu-west-1` deploy in [samconfig.toml](samconfig.toml))
   - `TracingEnabled: true` to match Lambda `Tracing: Active`
   - `Auth`:
     - `ApiKeyRequired: true` as the default on all methods
     - `UsagePlan`:
       - `CreateUsagePlan: PER_API`
       - `Description: "Usage plan for RTE Orchestrator Create API"`
       - `Quota` / `Throttle` set to sane defaults (e.g. `RateLimit: 10`, `BurstLimit: 20`, `Quota: 10000/DAY`) — RTE volume is low.
   - `MethodSettings`:
     - `LoggingLevel: INFO`, `DataTraceEnabled: false` (PII — never log request bodies), `MetricsEnabled: true`
   - Tags matching existing `samconfig.toml` tag scheme (application, team, product, etc.) — SAM auto‑tags from config, but explicit tags on `AWS::Serverless::Api` are fine.

3. **Attach Event source to `CreateFunction`**
   - Add `Events` block on the existing `CreateFunction` resource:
     ```yaml
     Events:
       CreateErasureRequest:
         Type: Api
         Properties:
           RestApiId: !Ref CreateApi
           Path: /erasure-request
           Method: POST
     ```
   - This auto‑wires the Lambda permission for API Gateway to invoke the function. No changes needed to `ServerlessAccessManager` / `LambdaExecutionRoleArn`.

4. **New `Outputs`**:
   - `CreateApiUrl` — the invoke URL (`https://{ApiId}.execute-api.${AWS::Region}.amazonaws.com/${Environment}/erasure-request`). Useful for configuring OneTrust.
   - `CreateApiId` — the API Gateway REST API ID (handy for debugging / console links).
   - Note: the API key **value** is intentionally not output; retrieve it from the API Gateway console or via `aws apigateway get-api-key --include-value`.

### No changes required to the Lambda code (in this change)

`lambda_handler` in [creator_lambda.py](src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py) already accepts `dict[Any, Any]`. When invoked by API Gateway (proxy integration), it will receive the standard API Gateway proxy event. `request_from_event` currently raises `NotImplementedError` — that remains a separately tracked TODO and is **out of scope** for this change. Callers will receive a 500 from the Lambda until that is completed; this is expected.

> If the reviewer wants a minimal safety net, we can return a `502`/`500` explicitly from the Lambda here, but per AGENTS.md "don't add features beyond what the task requires" — leaving as is.

### Changes to [TODO.md](TODO.md)

Tick off what this change delivers and add a line for API‑key rotation process if not already tracked:
- Add: `- [x] Add API Gateway for Create Lambda` under the SAM section.

### No changes to [.gitlab-ci.yml](.gitlab-ci.yml) or [Makefile](Makefile)

- `sam validate --lint` and `sam build` already run on `template.yaml` changes (see rules in `sam-validate` / `sam-build` jobs). They will pick up the new resource automatically.
- `Makefile` only builds Lambda artifacts — API Gateway needs no build step.

## Critical files

- [template.yaml](template.yaml) — main change.
- [samconfig.toml](samconfig.toml) — reference only; verify `Environment` parameter overrides still line up (`dev`, `prod`).
- [src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py](src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py) — no edit; confirms the handler signature works for API GW events.
- [TODO.md](TODO.md) — update checklist.

## Verification

1. **Local validation**
   ```sh
   sam validate --lint
   sam build
   ```
   Both must pass. `sam validate --lint` is also enforced in CI via the `sam-validate` job.

2. **CI**
   - Push branch `DAS_4125_API_gateway`, open an MR. The `sam-validate` and `sam-build` jobs must go green.
   - Manually trigger `sam-deploy-dev` from the MR pipeline.

3. **Post‑deploy smoke test (dev)**
   - In the AWS console, locate the new `data-rte-orchestrator-dev` stack → API Gateway → usage plan → retrieve the API key value.
   - Exercise the endpoint:
     ```sh
     curl -i -X POST \
       -H "x-api-key: <KEY>" \
       -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
       -d '{"id":"test-1","source":"manual","date_issued":"2026-05-01T00:00:00Z","erasure_details":{"email":"test@example.com"}}' \
       "https://<api-id>.execute-api.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/dev/erasure-request"
     ```
   - Expected (until `request_from_event` is implemented): HTTP `502`/`500` with Lambda error in CloudWatch. Absence of a `403` confirms the API key / auth wiring is correct.
   - A request **without** the `x-api-key` header must return `403 Forbidden` — confirms `ApiKeyRequired: true` is enforced.

4. **Observability**
   - Confirm API Gateway access logs / metrics appear in CloudWatch.
   - Confirm X‑Ray traces link API Gateway → Lambda (both have tracing enabled).

AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
Description: >
  Right to Erasure Orchestrator.
  Originally a replacement for the Tines Orchestrator, this stack primarily
  consists of 2 AWS Lambdas that communicate with DynamoDB

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Description: Environment
    Type: String
  DynamoDBTable:
    Description: The DynamoDB table to create new RTE requests in
    Type: String
  DynamoDBTableGSI:
    Description: The DynamoDB table GSI to query for RTE requests
    Type: String

Resources:
  ServerlessAccessManager:
    Type: Custom::ServerlessAccessManager
    Properties:
      ServiceToken: !Sub "arn:aws:lambda:eu-west-1:${AWS::AccountId}:function:paas-serverless-access-manager"
      FunctionName: !Sub 'data-rte-orchestrator-create-request-${Environment}'
      VpcExecution: "true"
      DatadogLogging: "true"
      Targets:
        DynamoDBTable:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Table: !Ref DynamoDBTable

  CreateFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Metadata:
      BuildMethod: makefile
    Properties:
      FunctionName: !GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.FunctionName
      CodeUri: ./
      Handler: dns_de_rte_orchestrator.creator_lambda.lambda_handler
      Runtime: python3.13
      Tracing: Active
      Description: Create RTE requests
      MemorySize: 256
      Timeout: 30
      ReservedConcurrentExecutions: 10
      VpcConfig: !GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.VpcConfig
      Role: !GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleArn
      Environment:
        Variables:
          REPOSITORY_TYPE: "dynamodb"
          DYNAMO_DB_TABLE: !Ref DynamoDBTable
          DYNAMO_DB_INDEX: !Ref DynamoDBTableGSI
          POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME: !Sub "create-erasure-request-${Environment}"

AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
Description: >
  Right to Erasure Orchestrator.
  Originally a replacement for the Tines Orchestrator, this stack primarily
  consists of 2 AWS Lambdas that communicate with DynamoDB

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Description: Environment
    Type: String
  DynamoDBTable:
    Description: The DynamoDB table to create new RTE requests in
    Type: String
  DynamoDBTableGSI:
    Description: The DynamoDB table GSI to query for RTE requests
    Type: String

Resources:
  CreateApi:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
    Properties:
      Name: !Sub 'data-rte-orchestrator-create-api-${Environment}'
      StageName: !Ref Environment
      EndpointConfiguration:
        Type: REGIONAL
      TracingEnabled: true
      Auth:
        ApiKeyRequired: true
        UsagePlan:
          CreateUsagePlan: PER_API
          Description: Usage plan for RTE Orchestrator Create API
          Quota:
            Limit: 10000
            Period: DAY
          Throttle:
            RateLimit: 10
            BurstLimit: 20
      MethodSettings:
        - ResourcePath: '/*'
          HttpMethod: '*'
          LoggingLevel: INFO
          DataTraceEnabled: false
          MetricsEnabled: true
      Tags:
        application: rte-orchestrator
        vertical: data
        owner: data
        team: de
        managed_by: aws-sam
        product: right-to-erasure

  ServerlessAccessManager:
    Type: Custom::ServerlessAccessManager
    Properties:
      ServiceToken: !Sub "arn:aws:lambda:eu-west-1:${AWS::AccountId}:function:paas-serverless-access-manager"
      FunctionName: !Sub 'data-rte-orchestrator-create-request-${Environment}'
      VpcExecution: "true"
      DatadogLogging: "true"
      Targets:
        DynamoDBTable:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Table: !Ref DynamoDBTable

  CreateFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Metadata:
      BuildMethod: makefile
    Properties:
      FunctionName: !GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.FunctionName
      CodeUri: ./
      Handler: dns_de_rte_orchestrator.creator_lambda.lambda_handler
      Runtime: python3.13
      Tracing: Active
      Description: Create RTE requests
      MemorySize: 256
      Timeout: 30
      ReservedConcurrentExecutions: 10
      VpcConfig: !GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.VpcConfig
      Role: !GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleArn
      Environment:
        Variables:
          REPOSITORY_TYPE: "dynamodb"
          DYNAMO_DB_TABLE: !Ref DynamoDBTable
          DYNAMO_DB_INDEX: !Ref DynamoDBTableGSI
          POWERTOOLS_SERVICE_NAME: !Sub "create-erasure-request-${Environment}"

**[TODO.md](TODO.md):**

The user is asking about why the VS Code IDE is showing errors on those lines. Looking at the diagnostics I've been seeing, the errors are like "Unresolved tag: !Sub", "Unresolved tag: !Ref", etc. These are from the YAML language server in VS Code which doesn't understand CloudFormation intrinsic functions by default.

The fix is to install the AWS Toolkit or CloudFormation extension which adds proper schema support for CloudFormation/SAM templates, or to configure the YAML extension to use the CloudFormation schema.

The user says they have AWS Toolkit installed but still seeing errors. Let me think about this. The AWS Toolkit extension for VS Code should handle CloudFormation intrinsic functions. But the errors are about "Unresolved tag: !Sub" etc. 

This could be because:
1. The file might not be recognized as a CloudFormation template - it needs to be detected as SAM/CFN
2. The YAML extension might be overriding the AWS Toolkit's schema association
